title: Commissioner of Income Tax v. Kelvinator of India Ltd.
legal_principles: Reopening of assessment requires tangible new material
Change of opinion not grounds for reassessment
summary: Supreme Court held that after the 2001 amendment to Section 147, the Assessing Officer cannot reopen a completed assessment merely on the basis of a 'change of opinion'. There must be 'tangible material' to form the belief that income has escaped assessment. This remains the leading case on Section 147 reassessment.
key_holdings: Post-2001, Section 147 requires 'tangible material' for reopening — mere change of opinion is not sufficient
Where assessment was made under Section 143(3), there is a presumption against reopening without new material
AO cannot act as a review authority on his own completed assessment
Distinction between 'formation of belief' and 'change of opinion'
key_quotes: After the 1989 amendment, the power to reopen is much wider, but one important safeguard is that there must be 'tangible material' to support the belief.
Reason to believe is not the same as reason to suspect.
why_important: The most-cited Supreme Court judgment on income tax reassessment. Prevents arbitrary reopening of completed assessments by tax department.
relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Very directly relevant — governs when Income Tax department can reopen completed assessments. Critical for tax planning.
